name: Salesville
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding on flat terrain near Gallatin Gateway. Collect Quartz, Agate, and Chalcedony with no permit required - up to 25 lbs per day.
description:
Salesville is a public BLM rockhounding site in the Gallatin Valley near Gallatin Gateway, Montana. This flat, accessible location offers excellent opportunities for collectors of all skill levels to find Quartz, Agate, Chalcedony, and Jasper specimens without any permit or fee requirement. The site sits at approximately 4,921 feet elevation in an area shaped by glacial and fluvial deposits from the Madison Range uplift.

The flat terrain makes Salesville an ideal destination for families and beginners. The relatively low elevation means the site is accessible during shoulder seasons when higher mountain sites remain snowbound. Rockhounding here involves searching exposed gravels, washes, and eroded banks where silica-rich minerals have concentrated over millennia.

Collectors should focus their efforts on drainage areas and wash bottoms where running water naturally segregates heavier minerals. Agate nodules and Chalcedony often weather out of host rock and can be spotted by their distinctive luster and banding patterns. Quartz crystals and Jasper fragments are common finds in the surface gravels.

As BLM public land, Salesville requires no permit and has no entrance fee. The 25-pound daily collection limit per person applies. This site is perfect for a few hours of casual collecting or as part of a broader rockhounding tour of southwest Montana.
history: The Salesville area lies within the northern Rocky Mountains province, where Precambrian metamorphic basement rocks and Paleozoic sedimentary sequences have been uplifted and exposed by Laramide tectonics. The Madison Range to the south generated significant alluvial and glacial deposits during the Pleistocene, scattering silica-rich gravels throughout the Gallatin Valley floor that contain the Quartz and Agate specimens found here today.
tips: Search in and around dry washes and eroded stream banks where water action has concentrated heavier minerals - these areas often show darker gravels and polished stones. Look for Agate by scanning the surface for translucent, banded pebbles with a waxy luster; Quartz crystals will appear glassy and often reflect sunlight distinctly. Bring a small shovel or geopick to expose fresh material in shallow cuts, and a 5-gallon bucket to manage your collection. Early morning visits reduce competition and provide better lighting to spot specimens.
terrain_notes: Flat, relatively open terrain at 4,921 feet elevation. Sagebrush and grassland vegetation typical of Montana valley floors. Easy walking footing on natural surface gravels and exposed banks. Parking areas consist of pullouts along access roads with no major obstacles. Distance from parking to productive collecting areas generally less than 0.5 miles of easy walking.
best_season: April - October. Late spring through early fall offers the best conditions; roads are clear, temperatures are moderate, and vegetation is minimal. Winter snow can block access and collect in high spots. Summer can be hot in the afternoon, so early morning collecting is recommended.
written_directions: From Gallatin Gateway, head south on Montana State Highway 191 for approximately 8 miles. Look for BLM public land markers and pullouts on the east side of the highway in the Salesville area. The GPS coordinates 45.588106, -111.208554 mark the center of productive collecting terrain. Multiple small pullouts are available along the highway; park in the most accessible spot and walk into the surrounding gravels and washes.
road_conditions: Montana State Highway 191 is paved and maintained year-round. Pullouts and access roads are unpaved but generally well-traveled and passable by passenger vehicles in dry conditions.
parking_notes: Small unpaved pullouts along Highway 191 near the site. Limited formal parking infrastructure; arrive early during peak season. Capacity approximately 2-4 vehicles per pullout. No facilities available.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or digging equipment. Pack out all trash. Respect private property boundaries - stay on marked BLM land.
rules:

Pack out all trash
Stay on BLM public land
nearest_services: Gallatin Gateway, approximately 8 miles north. Limited services; nearest full amenities (gas, food, lodging) in Bozeman, approximately 30 miles north.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and rockhounders. Unpaved pullouts and easy walking on natural gravels. No wheelchair access to productive collecting areas, but the flat nature of the site accommodates those with limited mobility better than most Montana rockhounding sites. Sturdy shoes recommended for walking over rocky ground.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding near Gallatin Gateway at Salesville?
faq.answer: Salesville is known for Quartz, Agate, Chalcedony, and Jasper. Agate specimens often show distinctive banding and translucent coloring, while Quartz appears glassy and can form clusters. Chalcedony typically occurs as rounded pebbles with a waxy luster. Most finds are small to medium-sized specimens in surface gravels and washes.

faq.question: Is there a rockhounding permit required for Salesville BLM land in Montana?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Salesville. As BLM public land managed under FLPMA, the site allows free personal-use collection up to 25 pounds per day with no permit fees or applications. This makes it one of the most accessible rockhounding sites near Gallatin Gateway.

faq.question: How much can you collect per day at Salesville rockhounding site?
faq.answer: You can legally collect up to 25 pounds of specimens per person per day under BLM regulations. This limit applies to personal use only; commercial collection is prohibited. Most casual rockhounders collect well under this limit in a few hours of searching.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding at Salesville near Bozeman?
faq.answer: The best season is April through October. Late spring and early fall offer ideal conditions with clear roads, moderate temperatures, and minimal snow. Avoid winter when access roads may be blocked by snow, and summer afternoons can be hot. Early morning visits in May through September are most pleasant.

faq.question: Is Salesville rockhounding site beginner-friendly and family-safe?
faq.answer: Yes, Salesville is excellent for beginners and families. The flat terrain, easy accessibility, and abundant surface specimens make it ideal for children ages 6 and up. No permit or fee is required, and the site requires minimal hiking experience. It is a perfect introduction to Montana rockhounding.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need for rockhounding at Salesville?
faq.answer: Basic equipment includes a small shovel or geopick for exposing fresh gravels, a 5-gallon bucket or backpack for carrying specimens, and sturdy hiking boots. A hand lens helps identify Agate banding and Quartz crystalline structure. Bring water, sunscreen, and a hat. Early morning visits provide better lighting for spotting specimens and cooler temperatures.
